title: Welcome to FIGN · how the federation works
hook: What FIGN is, how the federation works, and why your org's logo stays on everything it makes.
body:
FIGN is an umbrella, not a destination. Femmes aux Consoles in Cameroon, Bambina pan-Africa, Nexal, Juju Games, Ingage, and others are peers under it. Every event, workshop, and lesson carries its creator's brand — FIGN is never the default host.

That's the federation rule. When Bambina teaches foam armour, the lesson says Bambina. When Juju runs voice-acting drills, it says Juju. When a Digital Safety event ships with Alliance Française, the poster says Alliance Française. FIGN's logo sits beside theirs, not on top.

What this means for you as a member: your home org isn't replaced by FIGN, it's amplified. You can follow multiple orgs, join events across countries, and the federation is the thread between them — the shared map, the shared lesson library, the shared safety standards.

Your first week: pick three interests, follow your home org plus one other, and say hello in a play-night. That's onboarding.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Your map · how interests work
hook: Your map isn't a career track. It's what you love, plus what you've done.
body:
There are no stages on FIGN. No tracks. No levels. You pick interests — fighting games, voice acting, cosplay, streaming, community — and your map starts there.

The map is two things at once. What you love: the interests you tag in onboarding, plus any you write in your own words. What you've done: lessons you finish, events you attend, circles you help run. The two overlap in the middle, and that's the shape the system uses to suggest what's next.

You can pick three interests or fifteen. You can change them any time. The map redraws itself. Your home org — Femmes aux Consoles in Cameroon, Bambina across the continent, a FIGN chapter in Lagos or Nairobi or Accra or Yaoundé — stays where it is, and new paths open beside it.

The map is for you, not for a recruiter. Use it like a reading list you made for yourself, not a CV.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Welcoming newcomers · the first 10 minutes
hook: What to say when a woman opens FIGN for the first time.
body:
A new woman opens FIGN for the first time. The next ten minutes of attention from someone already inside decide whether she comes back.

Three sentences work. One — name yourself and your org. 'I'm Amina, FIGN chapter in Abuja' works as well as 'I'm Marie, Femmes aux Consoles in Yaoundé' or 'I'm Thandi, Bambina circle in Joburg.' Two — ask what brought her here. 'What got you curious — playing, creating, streaming?' Three — name the next thing. 'We've got a play-night Saturday; want the link?'

Then one real question. Not a checklist item. 'Which game have you been coming back to lately?' Listen. Don't pitch FIGN. She already signed up — the pitch worked.

Finally, a handoff. Introduce her to one member who plays what she plays or creates what she creates. A voice note is enough. Name them both in one message so neither has to do the work of finding the other.

That's the welcome. Ten minutes. One person. The federation feels real when one member shows up for another.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Hosting a play-night · the 2-hour run sheet
hook: By the end: a run sheet, a welcome script, a safety checklist.
body:
Two hours. That's the whole event. Longer and attendance drops; shorter and the room never warms up.

Structure: four 25-minute blocks, two 10-minute breaks, one 10-minute closing circle. Block one — low-stakes warm-up (Just Dance, a party title, anything that gets laughter in the room). Block two — the main game. Block three — rotate brackets so no one sits out. Block four — free-play and casuals.

Four things ready before anyone arrives. A run sheet on paper. A welcome script (three sentences you say aloud at the start). A safety checklist — who's the point of contact if something goes wrong, where's the girls-only cooldown corner. A photo-consent cue you actually ask out loud, not a form you hope someone signs.

Close with a circle. Each person names one moment they liked. Write those down. They become the next event's invite copy — real voice, not a template.

The number that matters is not attendance. It's how many women say 'when's the next one?' before they leave.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Finding a mentor · writing the first note
hook: Three sentences that make her say yes.
body:
Three sentences is enough to make someone say yes.

Sentence one — be specific. Not 'I'd love to pick your brain.' Try: 'I saw your post about running the Carry1st community, and the bit about moderating voice rooms really stuck.' Name the thing. If you can't name a specific thing, you're not ready to write to her yet — go read more of her work first.

Sentence two — offer something back. Even if small. 'I can help test your Discord onboarding flow.' 'I'll share what I try on my chapter's group.' Mentorship isn't extraction. Two-way, even uneven, beats one-way every time.

Sentence three — end with a real question, not an open invitation. 'Could we do a 20-minute voice note exchange next week — I send mine Monday, yours whenever?' Specific ask, small window, low cost to her.

Don't apologise for your message. Don't pile on credentials. Don't send 'just following up' after 48 hours. Two weeks is polite; a month is fine. If she doesn't reply, it's almost never personal — it's her inbox.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Digital safety · the essentials
hook: Your handle, your DMs, your recordings — choose before someone chooses for you.
body:
Five rules you can follow tonight.

One — handle. Pick one that doesn't link to your legal name, school, or phone number. Search it on three platforms before you commit. If it already belongs to a stranger, keep looking.

Two — DMs. Set them to mutuals or followers only. Public inbox by choice, not default. Voice-call invites from strangers blocked on every platform you use.

Three — two-factor auth on email, your main game account, Discord, and the phone number tied to them. Authenticator app over SMS if you can — SIM-swap scams are the fastest-growing account attack across African mobile networks, from Lagos to Accra to Nairobi to Cape Town.

Four — recordings. Before you stream or record a call, know where the file goes. A screenshot of you in a private lobby is a public object the moment someone else has it. Delete is not a feature of the internet.

Five — an ally. One person who knows your handles and hears from you after hard rooms. Safety isn't a checklist. It's a relationship.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Moderation for stream chats
hook: The three chat-mod rules every woman streamer uses.
body:
Three rules, three hotkeys, one trusted mod.

Rule one: no name-calling, no slurs, no personal info. Stated in the channel description and pinned as your first message of every stream. Repetition is how rules become norms.

Rule two: warnings are cheap, timeouts are automatic. First offence — a warning from the bot. Second — a 10-minute timeout. Third — a ban. No debates in chat mid-stream; debates are what the harasser wants.

Rule three: DMs are not a substitute for public behaviour. If someone escalates to your DMs after a chat ban, they stay banned. Screenshots go to your mod.

Three hotkeys on stream-deck or keyboard: timeout, ban, delete message. Know them without looking at the screen.

One trusted mod who is not you. They enforce while you play. You trust them to act without asking. Rotate them if it gets heavy — moderation burns people out.

References: Twitch Safety Center (safety.twitch.tv), Streamlabs moderation guide, Nightbot and Fossabot spam-filter templates.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Harassment · the escalation path
hook: From screenshots to platform reports to allies — a path, not a flowchart.
body:
Harassment isn't a flowchart. It's a path. Know who to tell and what to keep.

Step one — capture. Screenshots, not just memory. Usernames, timestamps, platform. Keep the originals even if you block the person five seconds later. A locked folder on your phone is enough.

Step two — tell one ally that night. Not three. One. The goal is not to carry it alone until morning. A voice note, a DM, a phone call. Someone who'll check in tomorrow.

Step three — report on the platform. Twitch, Discord, Twitter, in-game. Most reports go nowhere on their own; yours joins other reports and eventually does. File it anyway.

Step four — if it crosses into real-world threat (your address, your school, your family), save everything and contact local authorities. Most African jurisdictions now have cybercrime laws that criminalise online threats — Nigeria's Cybercrime Act (2015), Cameroon's Cybersecurity Law (2010), Kenya's Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act (2018), Ghana's Cybersecurity Act (2020), and South Africa's Cybercrimes Act (2020) all apply. Evidence is what unlocks a case, whichever country you're in.

Step five — tell FIGN. community@fign.org. We don't fix platforms, but we can help you escalate, find a lawyer, or just not be alone in it.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Reading MK1 frame data under pressure
hook: Drill the frames until they feel like muscle memory.
body:
Frame data is the math behind every 'why did I get hit.' You don't memorise it; you drill it until the frames feel like rhythm.

Four blockstrings to start. Each character has them. Open the move list, pick four strings your main uses most, note the startup, block advantage, and the gaps between hits.

Then go to practice mode. Record the dummy doing the string. Block it. Try to poke in each gap. Did your poke interrupt? If yes, that's your opening. If no, you're minus and must block out.

Now reverse it. You do the string on the dummy. Set the dummy to block-and-punish after the last hit. If the dummy's punish comes out, your string is unsafe on block. Change the ender or stop before the last hit.

The goal isn't to memorise a table of numbers. It's to train the gut — after block of X, your hands already know whether to press or wait. Twenty minutes a day for a week gets you there faster than ranked ever will.

References: Dustloop MK1 wiki, Kombat Academy frame-data tool, the move list inside training mode.

title: Punish optimisation · MK1
hook: The three punishes you'll quietly regret not learning early.
body:
The three punishes you'll quietly regret not learning early are d2, a high-low mixup, and corner carry. Learn them in that order.

D2 first. It's your universal launcher. When a string ends minus, or a jump-in is blocked, d2 catches it. Lab the exact range so you don't whiff. Then extend with the basic juggle your character gets — usually f-or-b string into special.

High-low mixup second. After a knockdown you get one big mixup. Drill the timing on a blocking dummy: 50% high starter, 50% low starter, from the same stance. The dummy will eat the wrong one about half the time. So will humans, and the ones who don't will still flinch.

Corner carry third. Every punish should end near the corner, not in neutral. Corner is where your damage doubles. Pick one combo per character that pushes to the corner even at the cost of 2–3% raw damage. It pays back within 30 seconds.

Twenty minutes each, three sessions, and you're ranked-ready with a kit most opponents at your tier haven't labbed. Mental note: winning isn't 'I must win to feel okay.' It's 'I executed what I drilled.'

By Sophia Nei.

title: Mental game · losing without tilting
hook: Lose on purpose. Then lose for real. Then keep playing.
body:
Losing is the job. Tilting is optional. Here's the reset.

After a bad set, stand up. Physically. Walk five steps. The point is to break the chair-linked loop your brain associates with the loss. Water, not a new queue.

Second, replay one thing that went wrong — not five. One round, one mistake, one correction. 'I kept pressing after block.' Done. Not a post-mortem, a note.

Third, lose on purpose. Queue a set and decide in advance you're testing one thing — a new normal, a new anti-air, a new punish. If you win, fine. If you lose, you got data. This breaks the 'I must win to feel okay' loop that turns ranked into a mood swing.

Fourth, a time cap. Ninety minutes max per ranked session. After that, casuals or offline. Rank is worst when you're exhausted, and your brain can't tell exhausted from tilted.

You don't win by never tilting. You win by noticing tilt five minutes sooner than yesterday, and standing up.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Voice acting for games · intro
hook: Cold-read a real script from Vodou: A Space Odyssey.
body:
A 45-word scene from Vodou: A Space Odyssey. Your warm-up. Your read. Your second read.

Warm-up first. Two minutes. Lip trills, jaw stretches, the five vowels loud then soft. Game voice-acting demands range from whisper to battle shout in one session. Your throat won't forgive you for skipping this.

First read — cold. Don't analyse. Don't 'prepare.' Record straight-through. The first take is where your instincts live; they disappear the moment you start thinking about what the director might want.

Second read — one note. Listen back to the first. Pick one line that felt wrong. Give yourself exactly one adjustment — slower, lower, angrier, quieter. Re-record the whole scene with that single change.

Don't re-record again. Send both takes to a peer or mentor. Ask: which feels more alive? That's your answer on the direction to take next session, not a confidence test.

From Juju Games · adapted by FIGN.

title: Cold-reading a script · practice
hook: The drill that makes cold-reads feel like warm-reads.
body:
Three scripts. Three takes each. No re-recording between the first and second.

Script one is quiet. A monologue, a confession, an intimate moment. Don't perform it. Speak it like it's 2am and you're tired. Mic close, breath audible, no push.

Script two is public. A speech, a command, a rally. Full voice. Stand up. Your diaphragm, not your throat, does the work — if your throat hurts after three reads, you're pushing from the wrong place.

Script three is conversational. Two characters talking. You read both. Shift weight on your feet between characters so your body marks who's speaking. The mic will hear the shift.

Rules during the session. First take: cold, no prep. Second take: listen back to first, give yourself exactly one adjustment, record. Third take: listen to first and second, try the approach you didn't pick. No re-recording between — the gap between takes is where learning happens.

By the end you'll have nine takes. Pick the three you'd send to a director. Delete the rest. That ruthlessness is the practice.

From Femmes aux Consoles · adapted by FIGN.

title: Cosplay sewing · basic stitches
hook: Running stitch, backstitch, whipstitch — the three that build a wardrobe.
body:
Running stitch, backstitch, whipstitch — the three that build a wardrobe.

Running stitch is the fastest. In, out, in, out, evenly spaced. Use it for basting (temporary holds while you check fit) and for long straight seams that won't see stress — the inside of a cape, the lining of a skirt.

Backstitch is strong. Forward-in, back-out, forward-in past the last stitch. It's what holds armour straps, jacket seams, waistbands. Slower, but it survives con-wear and the hours you'll spend in the costume.

Whipstitch closes edges. You wrap the thread around two edges in a diagonal, binding them. Use it for finishing hems, attaching patches, and closing the gap you left for turning fabric right-side-out.

One evening's practice: a finished hem on a scrap, a lined seam on another, and enough muscle memory that you stop dreading cutting fabric you can't replace. Cheap cotton first. Real fabric after, once the stitches are consistent.

From Bambina · adapted by FIGN.

title: Foam armour · starter pattern
hook: A pauldron, a forearm, a greave. Enough to cosplay a guardian.
body:
A pauldron, a forearm, a greave. Enough to cosplay a guardian.

Materials: EVA foam (6mm and 10mm), Barge cement (not hot glue — it fails at the first convention), a heat gun, a sharp utility knife, and black leather-effect contact paper for edges.

Pattern first. Wrap cling-film and masking tape around the body part you're armouring. Draw the shape on the tape. Cut it off. That's your flat pattern. Transfer to foam with a silver marker.

Cut with the utility knife held vertical — not angled, or your edges won't meet cleanly. Heat-form curves by warming the foam gently until it flexes, then hold the shape until it cools. Thirty seconds is usually enough.

Glue with Barge cement: coat both surfaces, wait two minutes until tacky, press firmly. It bonds in ten seconds and won't release — so dry-fit before you glue.

Prime with Plasti Dip or two coats of Mod Podge before paint, or the paint flakes off the first time you sit down. Finish with a matte sealer so it photographs without glare.

From Bambina · adapted by FIGN.

title: Shoutcasting 101
hook: The three sentences every caster masters first.
body:
Setup. Stakes. Surprise. The three sentences every caster masters first.

Setup tells the viewer where we are. 'We're in game three of a best-of-five. Tied 1-1. Map pick: Dust 2.' One breath. No hype yet — just facts so a first-time viewer can follow.

Stakes tell them why it matters. 'Winner takes the five-hundred-thousand-naira prize and a seed into the regional.' This is what turns a random match into a match the viewer stays for.

Surprise is the moment the match breaks pattern. A clutch, a throw, a reset. 'She's one-versus-three… and she's taken two of them!' Your voice climbs here, not earlier. Save the climb for when it's earned.

Combine: 'Game three, tied 1-1, five hundred k on the line — and Amaka is one-versus-three in the pit.' Twelve seconds of cast that gives a first-time viewer everything.

Drill by muting a VOD and re-casting the first minute. Five reps per session. It gets shorter and cleaner every time. Record yourself; listen back the next day.

From Ingage · adapted by FIGN.

title: OBS for new streamers
hook: From install to first 'Go Live'.
body:
From install to first 'Go Live' in under twenty minutes. A scene for desktop, a scene for game, a scene for your face.

Install OBS Studio free from obsproject.com. On first open, the auto-configuration wizard asks: streaming, recording, both. Pick streaming. It tests your upload and picks a resolution that won't drop frames. Trust it — tuning manually is a later problem.

Build three scenes. Desktop — a display capture of your whole screen, webcam in the corner. Game — a game-capture source locked to your active game, webcam in the corner, a chat overlay if you want one. Face — just the webcam full-screen, for breaks and intros.

Switch scenes with hotkeys (Settings → Hotkeys). F1, F2, F3 works. Your hands shouldn't need to leave the game.

Before you go live: record five minutes locally. Watch it back. Audio peaks in yellow, never red. Game capture is clear. Webcam isn't too dark. If any of those fail, fix them before an audience watches you fail.

Go live via Stream → Connect account (Twitch or YouTube). Title, category, tags. Click Start Streaming.

References: OBS Quick Start guide at obsproject.com/wiki, Twitch Creator Camp, Streamlabs setup docs.

title: Unity · your first scene
hook: Install to a scene with a moving cube in under 35 minutes.
body:
Install to a scene with a moving cube in under 35 minutes.

Download Unity Hub from unity.com/download. Install the latest LTS editor (Long Term Support — not the beta). The download is 4–6 GB; start it before anything else.

New project: 2D template, pick a folder, wait two minutes for Unity to create it. Don't rename folders after — Unity tracks files by internal GUID.

In the Hierarchy panel, right-click → 3D Object → Cube. The cube appears in the Scene view. Select it and look at the Inspector — the Transform (position, rotation, scale) is the game's memory of where it is right now.

Create a script. Project panel → Create → C# Script. Name it PlayerMove. Drag it onto the cube. Double-click to open in your editor.

Replace the Update() method body with one line: transform.Translate(Vector3.right * Time.deltaTime * 3f);

Save. Back in Unity, press Play. The cube moves right. That's a game loop — input (implicit), update (your line), render (Unity). Sixty times a second.

Stop Play. Change right to up. Try 5f instead of 3f. Feel how tweaking numbers changes the game. You've just built Layer 2 and Layer 3 of the five-layer model.

From IGDA Foundation · adapted by FIGN.

title: Godot · build a 2D platformer
hook: A playable platformer in under an hour.
body:
A playable platformer in under an hour. Scene tree, gravity, jump, collect, win screen.

Download Godot 4 from godotengine.org — 80 MB, no installer. New project: 2D, Forward+ renderer.

Scene tree first. Root: Node2D. Child: CharacterBody2D (your player). Children of that: CollisionShape2D (a rectangle sized to your sprite) and Sprite2D (drop any PNG — placeholder art is fine; you can swap it later).

Add gravity. Attach a script to CharacterBody2D. In _physics_process(delta), apply gravity when not on the floor. Godot has a built-in is_on_floor() helper so you don't have to track it yourself.

Add jump. Detect input 'ui_up'. If pressed and on_floor, set velocity.y to -400. Call move_and_slide() every frame so collisions resolve cleanly.

Build a level. Add a TileMap node. Paint platforms. Use a StaticBody2D with a CollisionShape2D for ground, or the built-in tile physics editor.

Add a collectible. Area2D plus a Sprite2D. Connect the body_entered signal to a function that queue_free()s the collectible and increments a counter.

Win screen. When the counter hits three, change scene to a 'You Win' label. That's a full loop — start, challenge, reward, end.

Sixty minutes. Play it. Send the exported build to a friend. That's a shipped game.

References: Godot Docs 2D platformer tutorial, GDQuest YouTube, Kenney.nl free art packs.

title: Narrative design · the shape of a beat
hook: One beat, three reveals, one reversal. The shape that makes a story feel alive.
body:
One beat. Three reveals. One reversal. The shape that makes a story feel alive.

A beat is the smallest unit of story — one scene, one conversation, one decision. Inside it, three things must be revealed: something about who this character is, something about what they want, and something about what stands in the way. Miss one and the beat is exposition, not story.

Then the reversal. The last of the three reveals must flip expectation. Not a plot twist — a shift. She wanted to run; now she'd rather stay. He said he trusted her; now he doesn't. Something moves, in the character or in the audience.

Try this on a Juju Games scene you haven't seen before. Read once. Name the three reveals. Find the reversal. If you can't find it, the scene is underwritten. Write a one-line note suggesting what to add — not a rewrite, just the missing piece.

Do this for five scenes in a row and you'll stop writing flat ones of your own. The shape starts showing up in dialogue you write after you close the doc.

By Sophia Nei.

title: The esports value chain · how the money actually flows
hook: Every naira in esports moves through four stages. Break one, the money stops.
body:
Every esports economy has the same four links. Player performance. Audience attention. Content assets. Brand investment.

Players create records — win rates, tournament history, an improvement arc. An undocumented player is economically invisible, no matter the talent.

Records earn attention. Size × depth × frequency. A weekly 8-team league with 30 regular viewers beats one loud tournament per quarter. Consistency compounds; hype doesn't.

Attention becomes content assets — stream metrics, clips, data, rivalries. No capture plan, no proof. Every event you run without one is money you burned.

Brands only pay when the chain is whole. 'What's my ROI?' is the only question they ask. Without numbers, you're not pitching — you're begging.

Walk any Nigerian esports org through these four stages and you'll see where the money stops. Lagos and PH have Stage 1 and Stage 2 — players and lobbies, plenty of both. Stage 3 is where most events quietly die: no stream capture, no clip pack, no stats sheet, so Stage 4 never lands. Fix the chain in order, and Stage 3 is almost always the cheapest win.

By Sophia Nei.

title: What a brand actually reads in your esports deck
hook: Sponsors don't read decks. They scan for four numbers. Here's which.
body:
A sponsor opens your deck. They look for four things. Find them in under ten seconds or they close the tab.

One — impressions. How many people see the logo, for how long, across how many surfaces. Stream, clips, venue, post-event recap.

Two — frequency. Is this a one-off or a recurring touchpoint? Recurring beats loud-and-once every time.

Three — audience fit. A women-led PH tournament means something to a brand chasing Gen-Z women in Nigeria that a generic gamer event does not.

Four — proof. Past numbers: attendance, stream peaks, mentions. If you have none, run a small event first and capture it. Then pitch.

One slide per number. That's the deck. A real PH-style example: 40,000 cumulative stream viewers across four weekend events, 68% female audience confirmed by exit survey, logo visible twelve minutes per match, with ten post-event clips that together outperform the live stream on Instagram. Four slides. A brand reads that and replies the same week.

By Sophia Nei.

title: The 5 layers behind every game you play
hook: Interface, logic, state, data, design — every game is these five layers.
body:
Every game you have ever played is five layers stacked. Knowing them changes how you see every game — and whether you could build one.

Interface is what you see. Competitive games optimise for clarity, never confusion. If you have to think about the interface, it failed.

Logic is what happens when you tap. Coordinate matching, timing, hit detection. Same math behind a PUBG bullet and a FIFA shot.

State is what the game remembers. Your score, your level, the pattern you're repeating. Crashes usually mean state got confused.

Data is what gets saved. Leaderboards, rankings, records. Without data there is no competition.

Design is why it feels the way it does. Randomisation, difficulty scaling, feedback loops. The science behind why you can't put it down.

Try this tonight. Open EAFC 25 or CoD Mobile on your phone. Point to the interface — the score, the minimap, the buttons. Name the logic — how your tackle registers, how a headshot gets counted. Find the state — your current health, current score, round timer. Find the data — the leaderboard, the ranked badge, your match history. Find the design — matchmaking, daily rewards, the way comebacks feel earnable. You just mapped a commercial game. You can map any of them.

By Sophia Nei.

title: Breaking into gaming and tech without code
hook: You don't need to code to work in gaming. You need skills, visibility, and proof.
body:
Gaming and tech aren't built only by developers. They're ecosystems — community, operations, partnerships, comms, growth. Products fail when any of these are missing.

Most people get blocked because they don't code, don't have foreign experience, or think they're too late. None of those are real blockers. What matters is whether you're contributing visibly.

The path isn't 'apply to jobs.' The path is: pick one role, build small proof, share it, start conversations. Do that for 30 days and you're no longer starting from zero.

This lesson is the opener. The next five walk you through it.

The people you think of as 'in gaming' mostly don't write code. They run the Discord that makes a game feel like home. They secure the brand deal that pays the salaries. They write the email that reopens a stalled partnership. Those are the jobs. They're real, they're paid, and they exist in African studios right now — Carry1st in Lagos and Cape Town, Maliyo, Kucheza, Juju Games, and the ops and comms teams behind every African esports broadcast you've watched.

By Sophia Nei, from 'Breaking Into Gaming & Tech Roles (Without Being a Developer)'.

title: The 5 non-developer roles gaming actually needs
hook: Community, biz-dev, coordination, ops, comms — five non-dev roles that run every studio.
body:
Five categories of non-dev roles show up in every studio — from Carry1st and Juju Games to Riot and Meta.

Community and events. Run online and in-person communities, organise tournaments, gather feedback. Need: communication, organisation, empathy, consistency. Entry: volunteer, moderate, organise.

Business development and partnerships. Build relationships, secure sponsorships, identify growth. Need: research, negotiation, relationships. Entry: community involvement, founder-assistant work.

Product and project coordination. Track tasks, communicate across teams, keep projects moving. Need: documentation, detail, clear comms. Entry: coordinate events or small projects.

Operations. Keep internal systems running. Need: structure, problem-solving, reliability. Entry: admin, program, or ops support.

PR, comms and content. Shape how the product speaks. Need: writing, storytelling, planning. Entry: social, content, media coordination.

You don't pick all five. You pick one to start.

By Sophia Nei, from 'Breaking Into Gaming & Tech Roles'.

title: Finding your entry point · skills to roles
hook: Your skills already fit somewhere. You just haven't named it yet.
body:
You don't start from zero. You start from what you already do well. Ask that instead of 'what should I learn?'.

Most non-dev skills fall into two buckets. Creative and communication: writing, speaking, community, content, events. Maps to community, PR, content, partnerships. Strategic and organisational: planning, docs, research, coordination. Maps to ops, project coordination, biz-dev support.

Write one line: skill you have → role it fits. Writing → PR, content, community. Organising events → community, ops. Research → biz-dev, product support.

Pick one role. Not your whole future. Just where to start. People move across roles all the time once they're inside.

One practical move: open LinkedIn, search for 'Carry1st', 'Juju Games', or 'Riot Games Africa', and read ten non-engineer job posts. Highlight every responsibility that maps to something you've already done — even unpaid, even at university, even on a WhatsApp group you once ran. That highlighted set is your entry point, written in the language industry already uses. Take it into your LinkedIn summary that same evening.

By Sophia Nei, from 'Breaking Into Gaming & Tech Roles'.

title: Building credibility without a job title
hook: Titles follow proof, not the other way round. Here's how to build proof this month.
body:
Job titles in gaming follow visible work. Not the other way round. Stop asking 'who will hire me?' and start asking 'what can I finish this month?'.

Volunteer strategically. Pick something where you can take ownership of one small thing and document the outcome. Avoid invisible, repetitive volunteer roles.

Finish small projects. An event plan. A community strategy for a game you love. A content calendar for a creator. A one-page case study of how a platform engages users. Clarity, structure, completion — in that order.

Document everything. A Notion page, a LinkedIn post, a short write-up with 'problem, what I did, what I learned'. Proof of thinking outweighs proof of results early on.

Readiness comes after action. Not before.

A concrete example. A woman in Accra wanted to move into community-management work. In six weeks she ran three small Discord events for a 40-person group of women learning Valorant. She wrote a one-page recap after each event — numbers in, numbers out, one thing she'd change next time. By week seven she had a case study. By week ten, a paid freelance engagement from a brand sponsoring one of the events. The title followed the proof, not the other way round.

By Sophia Nei, from 'Breaking Into Gaming & Tech Roles'.

title: Getting your first opportunity · the outreach
hook: Opportunities come from visibility, not applications. Here's the outreach that works.
body:
First gaming-and-tech roles rarely come from job boards. They come from communities, events, referrals, and direct outreach — all of which require you to be visible first.

Pick two or three communities. Online groups, Discord, LinkedIn conversations. Be useful and present. Don't be loud.

The outreach that works is specific, short, honest. Not 'are you hiring.' Try this instead: 'I've been following your work on [thing]. I liked [detail]. I'm exploring [area] and learning by [action]. Could I ask you one or two questions when you have five minutes?'

When they reply, your goal is to listen — not impress. Ask how they started, what skills mattered early, what they'd do differently. End by asking what you could support or learn from. That is where opportunities emerge.

Consistency beats intensity. Five thoughtful messages in two weeks beats fifty generic ones.

By Sophia Nei, from 'Breaking Into Gaming & Tech Roles'.

title: The 30-day plan to enter gaming or tech
hook: Four weeks from 'no idea' to 'in conversations' — the plan that works with limited time.
body:
Thirty days won't land you a role. It will land you clarity, a small proof, visibility, and momentum. Those are what lead to opportunities.

Week 1 — orient. Pick one target role. Name one skill you have, one you need. Join two or three communities. Clean up LinkedIn to focus on skills, not titles.

Week 2 — build proof. Finish one small project: an event plan, a community strategy, a content outline, an ops checklist. Document it in under a page. Finished beats impressive.

Week 3 — share and connect. Post the project. Reach out to five people in the role with a personalised message. Attend one event, virtual or physical.

Week 4 — follow up. Re-contact people from Week 3. Ask how you can support. Apply to a small number of relevant roles. Reflect on what worked.

Next month: repeat. Refine the focus, build one new piece of proof, deepen relationships. Progress compounds.

By Sophia Nei, from 'Breaking Into Gaming & Tech Roles'.

title: Running a Digital Safety esports event · a playbook
hook: How to run a two-day esports event that makes girls safer online, not just louder on stream.
body:
A Digital Safety esports event is not a normal tournament with a workshop glued on. The whole structure teaches something.

Format. Female-only brackets for two titles (CoD Mobile, EAFC 25) plus mixed brackets for all. The women-only brackets exist so girls try games they don't usually play. The mixed brackets invite boys who'll stand beside them.

Day one — physical qualifiers across CoD Mobile, FIFA, Scrabble, Chess, Just Dance. Day two — finals plus a digital-safety workshop, a 'Can games be safe for girls?' panel with youth and industry, a VR demo, a creativity booth, a schools feature, a ceremony.

Partnerships. Venue and prize pool from one org (Alliance Française). Workshop and community outreach from another (FIGN). Tournament ops from a third (Nexal). Every partner gets a chip. None becomes the face.

What you capture. Participant numbers (target 100), schools reached, media impressions, workshop attendance, stream peak. Those are the numbers that unlock next year's sponsor.

title: The imposter feeling in your first lobby
hook: That feeling your skill doesn't belong in this lobby is a signal, not a verdict.
body:
Your first ranked lobby, your first dev call, your first panel — the imposter feeling shows up in all of them. It is not evidence. It is just a signal that you're in a room with new stakes.

What makes it worse is being the only woman. African women, especially, often carry a second layer — you represent everyone who looks like you before you've spoken a word. That's heavy. Name it.

Three small moves help. One — keep a receipts file. Three concrete things you've done this year that were hard. Open it before hard rooms. Two — ship visible proof. A run sheet, a stream, a write-up. The file fills itself. Three — find one peer who gets the second layer. Voice-note each other after hard days.

The feeling fades with reps. Not with pep talks.

One more move that helps: say it out loud. In a voice note to a trusted peer, or even just to yourself in the mirror before the room. 'I'm nervous because the stakes are new, not because I'm wrong to be here.' Naming the feeling takes it out of the private folder where your brain stores it as evidence of inadequacy. The rest of you can go back to playing the actual match, giving the actual talk, walking into the actual room.

Adapted by FIGN from Women in Games WIGJ mentorship conversations and IGDA Foundation scholar reflections.

title: Safer streaming · the privacy basics no one teaches you
hook: Before you stream: six choices that decide whether someone can find your bedroom, your school, your name.
body:
Every streamer chooses what's visible. Women get targeted more, so the choices matter more. Make them on purpose, once, before you go live.

One — handle. Pick one that doesn't link to your legal name or school. Check it doesn't already belong to a public account elsewhere.

Two — location leaks. Cover windows. Mute voice notes. Don't show timezone clocks on overlays. Don't stream from a landmark visible outside your window.

Three — two-factor on everything. Twitch or YouTube, Discord, email, the phone number attached to your account. Use an authenticator app, not SMS, if you can.

Four — chat mods set up before the first stream, not after the first bad one. One trusted mod, three hotkey commands (ban, timeout, delete), a blocked-words list.

Five — DM rules. Requests off by default. Public inbox only. Voice-call invites from unknowns blocked.

Six — an escalation path. Who you tell when something crosses a line. Screenshots kept, not deleted. Platform reports filed. One ally DM'd the same night so you don't carry it alone.

References: Twitch Safety Center (safety.twitch.tv), Streamlabs streamer-safety guides, Women in Games WIGJ online-harassment resources.

title: Your first esports contract · the three clauses to read first
hook: Your first team contract arrives as a PDF. Don't sign yet. Read these three clauses first.
body:
Most first-team contracts in African esports are short, informal, and tilted. That's not always malice — it's that few local orgs have lawyers. Your job is to read three clauses before you sign anything.

Exclusivity. Who owns your game time? Does the contract say you can't stream other games, enter other tournaments, wear other jerseys? How long does that last? 'Exclusive for 12 months' and 'exclusive for this tournament' are completely different contracts.

Revenue split. Prize money, sponsor share, stream revenue, merch. Get numbers on paper. 'We split fairly' is not a number. 70/30, 60/40, 50/50 — all reasonable depending on who brings what. Zero to the player is not.

Exit. How do you leave? Notice period, buyout, what happens to footage and your handle. A contract without a clear exit is a trap with a ribbon.

Bring someone who's read one before. A lawyer if you can, an experienced player if you can't. Never sign same-day.

References: Esports Bar Association player-contract primers, IGDA contract-literacy materials, public ESL and BLAST player-agreement templates.

title: Grants and scholarships for African women in games
hook: Funding exists. Most of it is quiet. Here's where to look and what to apply with.
body:
Funding for African women in games is small but real. It hides in fellowships, residencies, and partner programmes — not in obvious 'grants for gamers' pages.

Where to look. IGDA Foundation scholarships (annual, covers GDC attendance). Women in Games WIGJ ambassador and mentor programmes. Epic MegaGrants for indie devs. Carry1st creator programmes for mobile. Goethe-Institut and Alliance Française cultural-tech grants in-country. Local: Co-Creation Hub, Ventures Platform community grants, occasional Flutterwave or Andela ecosystem calls.

What to apply with. A one-page proof — the event you ran, the game you shipped, the community you grew. Numbers where you have them. A clear ask: 'I need X (naira, cedis, shillings, rand — whichever is yours) for Y, delivered by Z.'

A rejection is information, not a verdict. Ask why. Reapply next cycle with that feedback built in.

References: IGDA Foundation scholars programme, Women in Games WIGJ ambassador scheme, Epic MegaGrants, Carry1st creator programme, Co-Creation Hub and Ventures Platform community-grants calls.

title: Starting a game-art portfolio with what you already have
hook: You don't need a studio laptop to start a game-art portfolio. You need five finished pieces.
body:
A studio looking at a junior artist's portfolio wants proof of finishing more than proof of talent. Five finished pieces beats fifty half-done sketches.

What to include. One character turnaround (front, side, back). One environment, interior or exterior. One prop set of three related objects. One UI frame — health bar, menu, dialogue box — done in a consistent style. One piece that shows process: thumbnails, silhouette, final.

How to show it. ArtStation is free and standard. Instagram is fine for reach but isn't the portfolio. Link ArtStation from your bio and from any FIGN or Bambina chapter channel.

What recruiters screen out. Inconsistent rendering. Anatomy that's wrong in the same way across pieces. No captions — they can't tell which were paid, which were fan-art, which were studies. Label every piece.

Start with five. Replace them as you get better. Six months from now, rotate out the weakest one.

References: ArtStation Learning portfolio guidance, IGDA Foundation mentor threads on junior portfolios, Carry1st art-test briefs.
